glossary.def: A nonzero direction that a matrix only stretches, never rotates: Av = λv. Its scale factor λ is the eigenvalue. Eigenvectors are the natural axes of a linear map and the principal axes recovered by PCA.

The scalar λ by which a matrix stretches its eigenvector, Av = λv. For a 2×2 matrix the eigenvalues sum to the trace and multiply to the determinant; they are positive for a positive-definite (convex) matrix.

The factorization A = UΣVᵀ that exists for every matrix: a rotation, an axis-aligned scaling by the singular values σᵢ, then another rotation. It generalizes eigen-decomposition to rectangular and rank-deficient matrices.

The number of genuinely independent directions a matrix produces — the dimension of its column space, equal to the count of nonzero singular values. Real data matrices are typically near low-rank, which is what compression and LoRA exploit.

The set of input vectors a matrix sends to zero, Ax = 0. Its dimension (the nullity) plus the rank equals the number of columns — the rank–nullity theorem. A square matrix is invertible exactly when its null space is just the origin.

The result that truncating an SVD to its top k singular values gives the best possible rank-k approximation of a matrix, with Frobenius error exactly √(Σ_{i>k} σᵢ²). It underlies PCA, image compression, and latent-factor recommenders.
A vector's Euclidean length, √(Σ vᵢ²) = √(v·v). The norm of (3,4) is 5. Its companions are the L1 norm (sum of absolute values, source of sparsity) and the max norm (largest absolute entry).

The point in a subspace closest to a given vector — its 'shadow' — leaving a residual orthogonal to that subspace. Projecting targets onto a design matrix's column space is exactly ordinary least-squares regression.

refs.cite: Strang, G. (2016). Introduction to Linear Algebra (5th ed.). Wellesley-Cambridge Press.
Golub, G. H. & Van Loan, C. F. (2013). Matrix Computations (4th ed.). Johns Hopkins University Press.
Eckart, C. & Young, G. (1936). The approximation of one matrix by another of lower rank. Psychometrika, 1(3), 211–218.
Pearson, K. (1901). On lines and planes of closest fit to systems of points in space. Philosophical Magazine, 2(11), 559–572.
Stewart, G. W. (1993). On the early history of the singular value decomposition. SIAM Review, 35(4), 551–566.
